Q1: I developed a pretty flexible study routine that worked around my other commitments. I'd typically dedicate about 18 hours a week to studying, but I spread it out based on what felt right. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time creating detailed notes and summaries from the course materials. My routine wasn't always conventional—sometimes I'd study intensely for a few days, then take breaks to process everything. I found that writing things out by hand helped me retain information better than just passively watching videos.
Q2: Honestly, my pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I didn't have a strict Monday-at-9am type schedule. Some weeks I'd study more in the evenings, other weeks I'd batch everything into longer weekend sessions. The flexibility worked for me because I could adapt to my energy levels and other responsibilities. Deadlines definitely influenced when I'd ramp up my studying, but I tried to stay somewhat consistent with at least reviewing my notes regularly.
Q3: I usually logged into the LMS when I needed to check for new announcements, download materials, or see if there were updates on assignments. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any discussion posts worth reading or responding to. I also used it to track what was coming up and to access the video lectures, though I often downloaded those to watch later. It became a habit to check in regularly—about 28 times throughout the course—just to stay in the loop.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost automatic for me, like checking email in the morning. I'd usually open it while having my coffee, just to see if anything new had been posted. The discussion forums helped make it feel more like a community space rather than just a content repository. Once I started interacting with peers there, I found myself logging in more naturally to continue conversations or see responses. It became part of my daily rhythm without me really planning it that way.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable—I watched over 246 minutes worth and often rewatched sections that were complex. But what really helped me learn was downloading the reading materials and creating my own written summaries. The discussion posts were also useful because hearing different perspectives from my 15 or so peer interactions helped solidify concepts. I appreciated resources that I could engage with actively, like materials I could annotate and transform into my own notes. Passive content was less effective for my learning style.
Q6: I'll be honest—I didn't complete all the assignments as thoroughly as I could have. My assignment completion rate was around 30%, which doesn't reflect my actual engagement with the material. Some of the smaller tasks felt redundant when I'd already mastered the concept through my own studying. I also sometimes skipped practice quizzes if I felt confident about the material after my own review. It wasn't that these resources weren't valuable, but I had to prioritize what would give me the best return on my time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it genuinely helped me understand something new or deepened my existing knowledge. I valued things that I could actively engage with—writing, discussing, problem-solving—rather than passive consumption. If I could see how an activity connected to the bigger picture of the course or to my exam preparation, I was more motivated to complete it. The activities that allowed me to test my understanding and get feedback were particularly worthwhile. Time investment had to match learning outcomes for me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were opportunities rather than obligations. I approached them with more curiosity and less pressure, which sometimes made them more enjoyable. Required activities could feel like checkboxes, especially when I'd already understood the material through my own methods. With optional ones, I could choose based on genuine interest or perceived benefit. That said, I probably should have completed more required assignments, but my exam performance showed I was learning effectively regardless.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened outside the LMS. I downloaded readings and lecture slides to study offline, often on my tablet during commutes or in cafes. I created extensive handwritten notes and summaries that I'd review repeatedly. I also used external resources like textbooks from the library and academic articles I found through other databases. My read-write learning style meant I was constantly writing, rewriting, and organizing information in ways that wouldn't show up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. For instance, I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or discussing concepts with study partners in person. I'd work through problems on paper, testing my understanding without any digital trace. Sometimes I'd listen to downloaded lectures while exercising or doing chores—definitely learning, but invisible to the system. My best thinking often happened away from screens, just processing and connecting ideas mentally.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard practice. I'd access materials once to download them, then study them extensively offline. The video lectures especially—I'd download them and watch them multiple times on my own device at different speeds. Reading materials got printed or saved to my tablet for annotation. This approach let me study anywhere without needing internet access and allowed me to engage with materials in my preferred way. One login might represent hours of subsequent offline studying.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became harder, particularly when stress levels peaked. I experienced high stress during the course, and sometimes that made it difficult to focus or motivate myself to log in. When I had competing responsibilities or felt overwhelmed, the LMS became less appealing. My attendance was around 50%, which reflects times when I chose self-study over synchronous participation. During those harder moments, I relied more on my own notes and offline materials to keep progressing.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd default to my comfort zone—reading my own notes rather than engaging with new LMS content. Workload from other areas of life meant I had to be strategic about where I invested my energy. Sometimes I'd log in with good intentions but feel too mentally drained to engage meaningfully with discussions or new materials. Competing responsibilities meant I couldn't always attend live sessions, which affected my attendance rate. I had to constantly triage what was most essential.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. High stress made it hard to be fully present sometimes. I'd find myself reading the same paragraph multiple times without absorbing it. In those moments, I learned to recognize that I wasn't being productive and would step away rather than waste time. It's why my actual learning often happened later, when I could focus properly on my downloaded materials and notes.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even knowing they were important. Sometimes it was because I felt confident I understood the material and the assignment seemed like busywork. Other times, stress made it easier to avoid tasks that felt overwhelming. I'd rationalize that my exam preparation was more important than individual assignments. Looking at my 30% assignment completion versus my 87% exam score, my prioritization worked academically, but I recognize I might have missed some learning opportunities through those assignments.
Q16: Deadlines created urgency that definitely spiked my LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, checking requirements and submitting what I could. They helped me prioritize when everything felt equally important. However, I also learned that not every deadline was equally critical—some assignments contributed less to my final grade, so I'd strategically focus on high-impact deadlines. The structure of deadlines helped me manage my time, even if I didn't complete everything.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when it was specific and constructive. Positive feedback on discussion posts encouraged me to participate more in those conversations. When I received feedback on assignments, it helped me adjust my approach for exams. However, delayed feedback sometimes meant I'd already moved on to new material. The feedback loop influenced my engagement—knowing my contributions were being read and valued made the LMS feel more like a real learning community rather than just a content delivery system.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, even if I didn't follow it perfectly. Having clear modules helped me understand the progression of concepts. The flexibility to access materials asynchronously was crucial given my attendance patterns. I appreciated that the course allowed different paths to success—my high exam score despite lower assignment completion shows the structure accommodated different learning approaches. The online courses I took alongside this one helped me develop strategies for navigating digital learning environments.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement much easier for my learning style. Having discussion forums created a sense of community that motivated participation. However, the assignment structure sometimes felt disconnected from what I found most valuable for learning. When activities felt like they were designed for compliance rather than understanding, engagement was harder. The video content was well-produced and engaging, which made those 246 minutes feel worthwhile rather than tedious.
Q20: I think high LMS activity with low submissions might reflect students who are genuinely trying to learn but struggling with the execution of assignments. They might be exploring, reading, watching—engaged with understanding—but facing barriers to completing formal work. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or prioritizing learning over grades. Sometimes the LMS activity is genuine engagement with the material, but the assignments don't capture what that student has actually learned.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was moderate, but my exam performance was strong because so much of my learning happened offline. Students like me might download everything early, then study extensively without generating more login data. We might have effective study strategies that don't require constant platform interaction. Low visible activity doesn't mean low engagement—it might mean efficient engagement, or engagement that happens through other channels entirely.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and completion of specific digital tasks. They capture the 'what' and 'when' of online behavior. But they miss the quality and depth of engagement—whether someone is truly thinking or just clicking. They can't see offline studying, peer discussions, or the mental work of processing and connecting ideas. They miss the 'why' behind behaviors and can't distinguish between productive struggle and disengaged scrolling. My 52% task completion doesn't reflect my actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and actively working to make sense of it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content—it's wrestling with ideas, asking questions, and connecting new knowledge to what you already know. For me, engagement looks like creating detailed notes, discussing concepts with peers, and thinking about the material even when I'm not formally studying. It's an internal state of curiosity and investment that might not always be visible externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline note-taking, which is where most of my real learning happened. It wouldn't capture the discussions with friends about course concepts, or the time I spent thinking through problems while walking or doing other activities. My resilience in managing high stress while still performing well academically wouldn't be visible. The CBT session I participated in helped me manage my stress, but that personal growth and its impact on my learning wouldn't appear in any log. My genuine intellectual engagement with the material exists beyond any digital trace.
